title: Unit 01 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Self-Assessment and Career Exploration.

questions.prompt: What does self-assessment help a student identify?

choices.text: Personal interests and strengths
choices.feedback: Correct. Self-assessment helps students recognize interests, strengths, and other personal traits.

choices.text: Only the highest-paying careers
choices.feedback: Self-assessment focuses on the student, not only salary.

choices.text: Only college majors
choices.feedback: Self-assessment is broader than choosing a college major.

choices.text: Only job application rules
choices.feedback: Job application rules are not the focus of self-assessment.

questions.prompt: Values can influence which careers feel like a good fit.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Values do affect career fit because they shape priorities and preferences.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Values help students decide which work and environments matter most to them.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes a career cluster?

choices.text: A single job opening at one company
choices.feedback: A career cluster is broader than one job opening.

choices.text: A form used to apply for a job
choices.feedback: Applications are not part of the definition of a career cluster.

choices.text: A group of related jobs and industries
choices.feedback: Correct. Career clusters organize similar occupations into larger categories.

choices.text: A list of school clubs a student can join
choices.feedback: School clubs are not career clusters.

questions.prompt: A student enjoys teamwork, helping others, and speaking clearly. Which is the best reason this matters for career exploration?

choices.text: It guarantees one final career choice immediately
choices.feedback: Career exploration supports decisions, but it does not guarantee one final answer.

choices.text: It means the student should ignore all other career options
choices.feedback: Students should explore options rather than ignore them.

choices.text: It removes the need to learn about career pathways
choices.feedback: Students still need to explore clusters and pathways.

choices.text: It gives evidence about careers that may be a strong fit
choices.feedback: Correct. Interests and strengths can support more thoughtful career matching.

questions.prompt: Work preferences can include the type of environment or schedule a student wants in a future job.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Work preferences often include schedule, environment, and the way a person likes to work.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Work preferences do include workplace conditions and routines.

questions.prompt: Which example best shows a career pathway?

choices.text: Health Science
choices.feedback: Health Science is a broad career cluster, not a narrower pathway.

choices.text: A more specific group of jobs within a larger cluster
choices.feedback: Correct. A pathway is a narrower set of related jobs within a broad career cluster.

choices.text: A list of personal hobbies
choices.feedback: Hobbies can connect to careers, but they are not pathways.

choices.text: A student's report card
choices.feedback: A report card is not a career pathway.

questions.prompt: What does it mean to match self to careers?

choices.text: Choosing a career based only on what friends like
choices.feedback: Career matching should be based on self-awareness, not peer choices.

choices.text: Selecting the most popular career without reflection
choices.feedback: Popularity does not determine career fit.

choices.text: Connecting interests, strengths, values, and preferences to possible careers
choices.feedback: Correct. Matching self to careers means comparing personal traits to career options using evidence.

choices.text: Ignoring values and work preferences during career planning
choices.feedback: Values and work preferences are important parts of career matching.

questions.prompt: Which factor should best support a student's career match?

choices.text: A random guess about what sounds interesting
choices.feedback: Career matches should be supported by evidence, not random guesses.

choices.text: Only what a student dislikes
choices.feedback: Dislikes can help narrow choices, but they are not enough by themselves.

choices.text: Only what a student can do in one school subject
choices.feedback: Career matching should use more than one factor.

choices.text: Clear evidence from interests, strengths, values, and work preferences
choices.feedback: Correct. Strong career matches are based on multiple pieces of personal evidence.
